facts.fact: Barcelona is hosting a Tour Grand Départ for the first time in the race's 123-year history — the Tour has only passed through the city three times before, in 1957, 1965 and 2009.
Barcelona last saw the Tour in 2009, when the race swept down for a rare finish in Catalonia — today's stage brings it back after 17 years away.
Neither Granollers nor Les Angles has ever hosted the Tour before — today's summit finish is a Pyrenean debut for both towns, and the race's first real GC test.

Carcassonne starts beneath a 12th-century fortified citadel, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Foix, today's finish, has become a happy hunting ground for breakaways — Luis León Sánchez, Warren Barguil and Hugo Houle have all won there since 2012.
Pau is the third most-visited location in Tour history, behind only Paris and Bordeaux — it has long been the organisers' reliable staging post for the Pyrenees.

Today's route crosses the Col du Tourmalet, first summited by the Tour in 1910 when Octave Lapize called race officials 'assassins' partway up, before finishing somewhere the race has never been: the Cirque de Gavarnie, a UNESCO-listed glacial amphitheatre.
Bordeaux was one of the Tour's original stage towns back in 1903 and remains the most-visited location outside Paris — today's finish is roughly its 82nd Tour appearance.
Périgueux and Bergerac both sit in the Dordogne, a region better known for wine and prehistoric cave art than cycling drama — expect the sprinters' teams to keep this one on a tight leash.

The 185 km through the Corrèze is relentlessly rolling rather than mountainous — around 3,400 m of climbing packed into repeated punchy rises, terrain that has often favoured breakaway survivors over the pure climbers.

Today's route crosses the Puy Mary, a striking volcanic summit in the Auvergne's Massif Central, before finishing at Le Lioran — the same ski station where Jonas Vingegaard beat Tadej Pogačar in the 2024 Tour.
Vichy has hosted the Tour only once before, in 1952, when Italy's Fiorenzo Magni won a time trial into the spa town from the Puy de Dôme.
Today's start line sits inside the Circuit de Nevers Magny-Cours, which hosted Formula 1's French Grand Prix from 1991 to 2008 — Michael Schumacher won there a record eight times.

The route climbs into the Jura foothills between two towns rarely visited by the modern Tour — a reminder that the race's path through eastern France is far less familiar to fans than its Alpine and Pyrenean stops.
Mulhouse sends the race into the Vosges, France's oldest mountain range, whose Ballon d'Alsace was the very first real mountain the Tour ever climbed, back in 1904.
Plateau de Solaison has never featured in the Tour before — today's summit finish, 11.3 km at 9%, is a complete unknown even to the riders' own team directors.
This 26 km time trial along the shore of Lake Geneva is the only individual time trial of this Tour — a far cry from the longest ever raced, a 139 km individual test back in 1947.
Voiron returns to the Tour route sitting at the foot of the Chartreuse massif — a rare flat stage sandwiched between two brutal Alpine blocks.
Orcières-Merlette last hosted the Tour in 1971, when Luis Ocaña rode alone for the final 60 km to demolish Eddy Merckx by almost nine minutes — one of the most dominant rides in Tour history.

Alpe d'Huez's 21 hairpins have decided Tours since 1952, when Fausto Coppi won the very first summit finish in the race's history. Today is the first of back-to-back visits to the climb — a Tour first.

This is the Tour's second straight finish atop Alpe d'Huez. The mountain has been climbed twice in one stage before (2013), but never used as back-to-back summit finishes on consecutive days until now.

Every Tour since 1975 (bar Paris-Olympics-affected 2024) has finished on the Champs-Élysées — including 1989, the only time the yellow jersey changed hands there, when Greg LeMond overturned a 50-second deficit to beat Laurent Fignon by just 8 seconds.
